title: Availability, Disruption Budgets, Graceful Draining, Autoscaling, and Capacity · Reconciliation Brief: K8s Pdb Voluntary Disruption
summary: A two-zone service scales from noisy metrics, violates its recovery target during drain, and has enough replicas but insufficient failure-domain and resource headroom. Planned artifact: Add and verify the k8s pdb voluntary disruption increment in availability and autoscaling operating policy. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Calculate minAvailable or maxUnavailable, unhealthy eviction policy, controller scale, simultaneous drains, and voluntary versus involuntary disruption limits.
Translate service objectives into replica count, failure domains, topology placement, readiness, surge capacity, dependency limits, and recovery evidence.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.config:
# kubernetes-basics cumulative orchestration portfolio
# Browser labs perform deterministic manifest and evidence review only.
# They never invoke kubectl, read kubeconfig, contact a cluster API, or execute host commands.
workspace: kubernetes
version-baseline:
  kubernetes: 1.36.2
  minikube: 1.38.1
  gateway-api: 1.5.1
  helm: 4.2.2
transfer-boundary: authorized-disposable-cluster
evidence:

steps.title: Predict K8s Pdb Voluntary Disruption · Theory K8s Pdb Voluntary Disruption
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory service has 4 replicas but all share one zone, a strict disruption budget blocks drain, and HPA oscillates because requests and metrics are wrong. Balance failure domains, PDB, rollout surge, autoscaling signals, stabilization, and capacity. Competency probe: Calculate minAvailable or maxUnavailable, unhealthy eviction policy, controller scale, simultaneous drains, and voluntary versus involuntary disruption limits.

content.title: Worked case pattern
content.text: A worked example service has 4 replicas but all share one zone, a strict disruption budget blocks drain, and HPA oscillates because requests and metrics are wrong. Balance failure domains, PDB, rollout surge, autoscaling signals, stabilization, and capacity. Competency probe: Calculate minAvailable or maxUnavailable, unhealthy eviction policy, controller scale, simultaneous drains, and voluntary versus involuntary disruption limits. A defensible solution applies K8s Pdb Voluntary Disruption, preserves the governing invariant or value contract, and connects the observed result to availability and autoscaling operating policy.

content.code:
# Evidence: k8s-worked-k8s-availability-disruption-autoscaling-4
  - competency: k8s-worked-k8s-availability-disruption-autoscaling-4
    artifact: availability-policy.yaml
    decision: worked k8s availability disruption autoscaling 4
    environment: air-gapped release cluster using an internal registry mirror
    fault-injection: drop one metrics series during an autoscaling surge
    retained-proof: accessible incident timeline with causal regression evidence
    case-key: worked4
    change: |
      objective: 99.9-percent-availability
      replicas: 4-across-2-zones
      pdb: minAvailable-3
      hpa: autoscaling-v2-cpu-and-queue
      stabilization: bounded
      node-headroom: measured
      faults: drain-zone-loss-metrics-gap
    verify: kubectl get deployment,pdb,hpa -n intake -o json
    failure: Reject replica-count-only HA, PDB as involuntary-failure protection, limit-based HPA math, or autoscalers without capacity and feedback analysis.
    repair: Tie replicas and topology to objectives, calculate disruption and scaling, reserve headroom, inject lag and failure, and measure user-visible service.
content.caption: Runnable Kubernetes object and transfer-evidence contract example; predict its result before running, then change one boundary input.

content.title: Non-worked case
content.text: A PodDisruptionBudget prevents node failures and guarantees that applications remain available. This fails the K8s Pdb Voluntary Disruption contract because it omits a precondition, invariant, ownership rule, or counterexample needed by this exact case.
